rights.basis: All five installments were published before 1929 and are in the United States public domain by publication date.
rights.component: MacCracken's 1911-1913 article texts and editorial matter as represented in CME00109

rights.basis: MacCracken died in 1970, so authorial matter may remain protected through 2040 in life-plus-70 jurisdictions even though the publications are public domain in the United States. Country-specific rules and exceptions require separate assessment.
rights.component: MacCracken's editorial introductions, notes, selection, and other authorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: Jurisdictions applying life plus 70 years or other non-US terms

next_steps: Review post-DIMEV attribution scholarship item by item.
Treat Stow's added names, MacCracken's stylistic comparisons, and manuscript rubrics as scoped evidence rather than blanket authorship.
Preserve John Marion's signed ABC as the only currently confirmed named authorship in the selection.
openQuestions.question: Which anonymous Lydgatiana poems, if any, can be assigned securely to John Lydgate or another identifiable author beyond John Marion?

next_steps: Compare Add MS 31042 directly with the Latin and Middle English prose traditions.
Reassess northern and possible Scottish linguistic claims using current scholarship.
Do not turn stylistic proximity to Lydgate into an attribution.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual route, date, and region produced the unique verse Three Kings of Cologne?

next_steps: Ask CUL to document the Findern old/current foliation concordance used by CUDL and DIMEV.
Confirm Arundel 249 and Cotton Caligula A II boundaries against new images.
Treat Harley 2250 in V.10 and the Arundel 168 'fol. 168v' milestone as editorial errors unless contrary primary evidence appears.
openQuestions.question: Can all legacy/current foliation and shelfmark conflicts be confirmed from institutional collation statements?

next_steps: Ask first whether an existing preservation surrogate can be delivered; request the complete current physical codex rather than only target folios when feasible.
In every request identify the target work folios separately so the full object can be mapped back to CME00109.
Obtain written repository-display, scholarly-publication, commercial print-on-demand, and redistribution terms for the exact delivery.
openQuestions.question: Can complete reusable whole-manuscript facsimiles be obtained for the British Library and Leiden witnesses and the three only-partially-imaged Bodleian witnesses?

next_steps: Keep source-cache files Git-ignored and publish URLs, checksums, download status, and work locators rather than cached image bytes by default.
Treat Bodleian, CUDL, and Trinity CC BY-NC images as noncommercial and obtain separate permission before any commercial reuse.
Seek item-specific clarification from Huntington and exact written terms for any newly supplied BL, Bodleian, CUL, or Leiden images.
openQuestions.question: Which cached manuscript image bundles may be redistributed through a repository that also supports commercial print-on-demand use?

relations.objectLabel: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English encoding CME00109

object.description: The twenty-six medieval verse units, excluding MacCracken's modern introductions

subject.description: Twenty-seven records representing twenty-six historical textual units; the Barnet selection has separate song and quatrain records

relations.subjectLabel: DIMEV v1.0 records for the twenty-six Lydgatiana textual units

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is MacCracken's/U-M's edited compilation boundary. It must not be presented as one medieval work or as wholly authored by Lydgate.

Complete manuscript facsimile means the complete sequence exposed by the provider's IIIF manifest, not proof that omitted physical surfaces do not exist. Every complete local bundle includes a work_portion mapping.

The full five source serial volumes, full HM 140, Tanner 346, Ff.1.6, Trinity R.3.21, and Kk.1.6 provider objects, three Bodleian selected-image objects, and DIMEV v1.0 are cached under gitignored source-cache/CME00109 with exact source URLs, byte counts, and checksums.
Public access does not imply permission for redistribution or commercial print-on-demand. The manifest deliberately separates access status, copyright assessment, exact licenses, and provider terms.

DIMEV has twenty-seven relevant records because the Battle of Barnet selection comprises a song and a separate following quatrain; the metadata manifest retains twenty-six historical textual units at the edited-compilation level.
Internet Archive's generic metadata dates for these serial volumes were not used; publication years come from inspected title pages and MacCracken's printed articles.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies the University of Michigan CME encoding of Henry Noble MacCracken's five serial Lydgatiana installments (1911-1913). Those installments edit twenty-six distinct Middle English verse texts from fifteen direct manuscript witnesses, sometimes with collateral witnesses. This record keeps the repository artifact, U-M encoding, five articles, serial-volume scans, physical manuscripts, institutional facsimiles, and DIMEV descriptions separate and records work folios inside every cached complete manuscript facsimile.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts chiefly fifteenth century; serial edition 1911-1913
catalogSummary.region: England; individual witnesses and one uncertain northern/Scottish hypothesis are scoped below
catalogSummary.title: Lydgatiana

catalogingSubject.label: Lydgatiana, installments I-V
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Henry Noble MacCracken's five-installment scholarly compilation Lydgatiana as encoded by U-M and preserved in CME00109: twenty-six distinct Middle English verse texts plus MacCracken's modern editorial introductions. The poems, their manuscript versions, MacCracken's serial articles, the U-M encoding, and this repository artifact remain distinct layers. The title does not make John Lydgate author of the compilation's anonymous poems.

contentParts.label: I. The Life of Holy Job
contentParts.summary: The 1911 first installment introduces and prints one twenty-six-stanza Job poem from former Phillipps 8299, now Huntington HM 140.

contentParts.label: The Life of Holy Job
contentParts.summary: A twenty-six-stanza narrative of Job's prosperity, trials, patience, death, and salvation; MacCracken prints it from the unique HM 140 witness.

contentParts.label: II. Two Chaucerian Ballades
contentParts.summary: The 1911 second installment prints two anonymous complaints from Tanner 346 and reports variants from the later Findern copy.

contentParts.label: As ofte as syghes ben in herte trewe
contentParts.summary: A thirteen-stanza lover's complaint with the refrain 'So ofte, and ofter, I sygh for youre sake.'

contentParts.label: Compleynt for Lac of Sight
contentParts.summary: A sixty-eight-line complaint arranged by MacCracken as a triple ballade with envoy, lamenting the beloved's absence.

contentParts.label: III. The Three Kings of Cologne
contentParts.summary: The 1912 third installment introduces and prints the unique, acephalous Middle English verse version from the London Thornton manuscript.

contentParts.label: The Three Kings of Cologne
contentParts.summary: The unique verse retelling of the Magi legend, surviving without roughly its first hundred lines and beginning within the journey narrative; it has 123 rhyme-royal groups in the encoding.

contentParts.label: IV. Unprinted Texts from Trinity R.3.21
contentParts.summary: The 1913 fourth installment prints eight poems from one Trinity manuscript and warns against John Stow's added Lydgate ascriptions.

contentParts.label: Life of St Anne
contentParts.summary: A ninety-four-stanza sacred life beginning with a prologue and transmitted anonymously despite Stow's added Lydgate label.

contentParts.label: Psalms of the Passion
contentParts.summary: A sequence of Passion meditations headed Incipiunt Psalmi Passionis domini.

contentParts.label: A Devout Prayer toward Bed at Night
contentParts.summary: A six-stanza nightly prayer for protection, contrition, and a good end.

contentParts.label: A Devout Prayer at Thy Uprising
contentParts.summary: An eight-stanza morning prayer beginning 'Jesu Lord blessed Thou be.'

contentParts.label: A Lament of Our Lady at the Passion
contentParts.summary: A fifteen-stanza Marian lament at the Cross; the Lydgate attribution added in Stow's hand is not accepted as authorship.

contentParts.label: An Exhortation to Avoid the Seven Sins
contentParts.summary: Ten verse groups exhort against pride, wrath, envy, sloth, gluttony, lust, and avarice.

contentParts.label: A Song on the Battle of Barnet
contentParts.summary: A thirty-one-line political song on the Yorkist victory at Barnet followed by a four-line aphoristic quatrain, represented by two DIMEV records.

contentParts.label: The Birds' Matins

locators.value: base copy ff. 196v-197r, canvases 398-399; duplicate ff. 246r-v

contentParts.summary: A ten-stanza avian devotional dialogue; MacCracken transcribes the first R.3.21 copy, while the manuscript contains a second copy at ff. 246r-v.

contentParts.label: V. Fourteen Short Religious Poems
contentParts.summary: The final 1913 installment gathers fourteen largely anonymous devotional poems from eleven codices, with strong Marian emphasis.

contentParts.label: Hoc Factum Est a Domino
contentParts.summary: A twelve-stanza poem that redirects scholastic and historical questions toward faith, including Agincourt and the 1436 Calais and Roxburgh campaigns.

contentParts.label: A Prayer to Christ's Name

locators.value: provider label f. 124v; DIMEV current f. 146v; canvas 252

contentParts.summary: A three-stanza prayer to Jesus' name; direct image inspection reconciles MacCracken's provider folio 124v with DIMEV's current folio 146v.

contentParts.label: Have Mercy on Me
contentParts.summary: A five-stanza penitential prayer beginning 'Almighty God maker of heaven.'

contentParts.label: Salve Regina
contentParts.summary: A four-stanza Marian prayer beginning 'Salve with all obeisance to God in humblesse.'

contentParts.label: All Hail Mary
contentParts.summary: A Marian praise poem of forty-seven encoded lines.

contentParts.label: Prayers to Mary and the Saints
contentParts.summary: A nine-stanza petition to Mary and named saints; the current DIMEV boundary is broader than MacCracken's printed locator.

contentParts.label: Lenvoy to Mary
contentParts.summary: A six-stanza Marian envoy beginning 'Go little bill and do me recommend.'

contentParts.label: Regina Celi Letare
contentParts.summary: A four-stanza Marian hymn with the Latin opening as refrain and rubric.

contentParts.label: The Five Joys of the Virgin
contentParts.summary: Five rhyme-royal groups on Mary's joys; MacCracken chooses the Cotton version over the poorer Ashmole 59 copy.

contentParts.label: Maria Virgo Assumpta Est
notes: Harley 2250 is retained as a documented printed shelfmark error, not modeled as the source witness.
contentParts.summary: A ten-stanza Marian poem with Latin refrain; MacCracken's headnote says Harley 2250, but the contemporary description, DIMEV, and the poem's location support Harley 2251.

contentParts.label: An Acrostic on MARIA
contentParts.summary: A fifteen-stanza Marian acrostic beginning 'Away faint love full of variance.'

contentParts.label: John Marion's ABC to the Virgin
contentParts.summary: A twenty-two-stanza alphabetic Marian poem explicitly signed by John Marion; a printed folio milestone in CME confuses the shelfmark 168 with a folio number.

contentParts.label: An ABC to the Virgin from a Leyden Manuscript
contentParts.summary: A twenty-seven-stanza rhyme-royal alphabetic hymn to Christ and the Virgin from Leiden Vossius Germ. Gall. Q.9.

contentParts.label: O Flos Pulcherrime
contentParts.summary: A twenty-two-stanza Marian prayer beginning 'Might, wisdom, goodness of the Trinity.'

scope.description: The twenty-six historical texts, not MacCracken's framing

notes: MacCracken's dating rests primarily on meter and language; manuscript dates are not composition dates.

dateStatements.display: first half of the fifteenth century, often placed about 1450

notes: The poem names Agincourt and the 1436 campaigns at Calais and Roxburgh.

dateStatements.display: after the Battle of Barnet, 14 April 1471

extent.description: The cataloged historical selection contains twenty-six verse works or fragments grouped under five installment sections: 181, 171, 859, 1,176, and 1,118 historical verse lines respectively, totaling 3,505 L elements in 491 LG elements. The XML has 3,509 L elements overall because four additional quoted verse lines occur in MacCracken's modern prose introductions. It also contains five introduction divisions, 53 P, 80 PB, and 125 NOTE elements; those representation features are not silently added to the medieval-item extent.

formStatements.description: A modern prose-framed scholarly compilation whose historical contents are verse

scope.description: Job, Three Kings, Trinity religious texts, and the fourteen short religious poems
Rubrics, refrains, headings, and devotional phrases occur within several poems

notes: The five parent parts model MacCracken's serial editorial installments; their twenty-six children model the historical textual units selected within them.
The historical selection has 3,505 verse lines. The XML's four additional L elements are quotations embedded in modern editorial introductions and are excluded from historical-work extent.

The Battle of Barnet item contains a song plus a following four-line aphoristic quatrain represented by separate DIMEV records; they remain one MacCracken numbered item with an internal two-work caveat.
Complete-facsimile cache status and exact download links are recorded in the linked lineage manifest, not inferred from catalog availability.

next_steps: Review post-DIMEV attribution scholarship item by item.
Treat Stow's added names, MacCracken's stylistic comparisons, and manuscript rubrics as scoped evidence rather than blanket authorship.
Preserve John Marion's signed item as the only currently confirmed named authorship.
openQuestions.question: Which of the anonymous Lydgatiana poems, if any, can be assigned to John Lydgate or to identifiable authors beyond John Marion?

next_steps: Compare Add MS 31042 directly with Latin and Middle English prose versions.
Reassess the northern and possible Scottish linguistic claims.
Do not convert MacCracken's stylistic proximity to Lydgate into an attribution.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual route, date, and region produced the unique verse Three Kings of Cologne?

next_steps: Ask CUL to document the Findern old/current foliation concordance used by CUDL and DIMEV.
Confirm Arundel 249 and Cotton Caligula A II boundaries against images.
Treat Harley 2250 in MacCracken's item V.10 as a printed error unless contrary primary evidence appears.
Keep the spurious Arundel '168v' milestone separate from the true f. 1 locus.
openQuestions.question: Can all legacy/current foliation and shelfmark conflicts be confirmed from institutional collation statements?

next_steps: Request complete whole-object reproductions by shelfmark, separately identifying work folios.
Ask for written repository-display, scholarly-publication, and commercial print-on-demand terms.
Do not treat a catalog page or selected-image IIIF object as a complete manuscript facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can complete reusable facsimiles be obtained for the British Library, Leiden, and only-partially-imaged Bodleian witnesses?

scope.description: Broad origin of the Middle English contents

notes: MacCracken inferred a northern author or linguistic setting; this remains a hypothesis.
Possible Scottish origin is reported in later discussion but is not established.
The poem concerns the 1471 Battle of Barnet and London political news; this is not a claim of composition place.

scope.description: All twenty-six medieval items except John Marion's ABC; several items carry rejected or uncertain historical ascriptions

notes: MacCracken explicitly rejects Stow's authority for the Trinity attributions and presents the two ballades and fourteen religious poems as anonymous works under Lydgatian influence.

scope.description: Historical Lydgate ascriptions or inclusion in a Lydgatiana context, especially John Stow's added names in Trinity R.3.21
Electronic encoding of MacCracken's five installments

subjects.label: Biblical Magi and the Three Kings of Cologne
Job as an example of patience

summaries.value: Lydgatiana is Henry Noble MacCracken's five-part gathering of twenty-six Middle English poems that he considered relevant to the study of John Lydgate without treating the collection as a secure Lydgate canon. It ranges from a Life of Job and a fragmentary verse Three Kings of Cologne through courtly complaints, a Life of St Anne, Passion and daily prayers, a Battle of Barnet song, and fourteen short religious poems dominated by Marian devotion. CME00109 retains the serial installments' modern introductions and their medieval texts. Its direct manuscript path crosses fifteen codices; John Marion is named only for item V.12, while the remaining authors are anonymous or disputed.

titles.value: Twenty-six Middle English poems edited under the title Lydgatiana

work.summary: Lydgatiana is Henry Noble MacCracken's five-part gathering of twenty-six Middle English poems that he considered relevant to the study of John Lydgate without treating the collection as a secure Lydgate canon. It ranges from a Life of Job and a fragmentary verse Three Kings of Cologne through courtly complaints, a Life of St Anne, Passion and daily prayers, a Battle of Barnet song, and fourteen short religious poems dominated by Marian devotion. CME00109 retains the serial installments' modern introductions and their medieval texts. Its direct manuscript path crosses fifteen codices; John Marion is named only for item V.12, while the remaining authors are anonymous or disputed.
